## [00:00 - 03:01]

Alright, so let's talk about scaling and how this actually should be done in order to increase the results that you're getting for an ad campaign. So there's really two main approaches that we would use to scaling, and the terms that have been coined around this is horizontal and vertical scaling. Most people are really familiar with the vertical scaling method, and that method is basically where you're simply finding the winning ad set, so the ad set that is producing the best result for you, and you're increasing the budget, the daily budget, by 16 to 20 percent every two to three days. So you can do it by every day. However, we like to allow things to just settle in, and we found a really sweet spot with about two to three days. So obviously, as you can see with the graph above, using this method is a much slower way to scale. However, it is definitely a safer way to scale. So you basically just increase that budget every two to three days until you start to see potentially the results dipping a little bit, and then you scale it back down. And if you find that when you scale it back down, it doesn't get that result back to what you wanted. The solution usually to that is to duplicate that ad set and get a new one up and going, and normally that will pick right back up where the other one was. So this is the slowest way to scale, but it's also the safest way. And when you have a client who is extremely sensitive with their CPA, so their cost per acquisition, or that metric is what they're really, really focused on, they want to make sure that they get the best cost per acquisition, then this is normally the scaling strategy that you would use here. Now the second strategy is called horizontal scaling, and that's the one on the left hand side there. And you can see with the graph that it is a very quick way to essentially double, triple, quadruple your budget, essentially almost even in the same day within the same 24 hour window. So if you have a client who needs to scale rapidly, so for example maybe they have, there's a Christmas sale or there's a last minute sale that needs to, you know, that is expiring in 48 hours, or there's a Kickstarter campaign or something like that, or they're doing really, really well with the campaigns, and they just need to put way more revenue through other business, and there's a little bit of tolerance with their CPA, then this method can be quite good. And what it does here is you're simply duplicating the winning ad sets, so the top performing

## [03:01 - 06:02]

ad sets that you have, you're duplicating them. Now how do you know which one is best to use between the two? So normally this is kind of a rule of thumb. If you're running vertical scaling, this is normally when you're still in the validation phase and or your client isn't in a big rush, and scale isn't the most important thing, so CPA is really important. And typically you're using auto bidding here. If you are in the horizontal, if you are scaling horizontally, sorry, then normally you can, you should only be doing this once you have validated the soft stats of that ad set, meaning that ad set has settled into a really good CTR, a really good CPC, you're getting great results from it, right? And then you can test it out with the horizontal scaling method, and usually this is using manual bid as well. So you would essentially create duplications, and I'm going to show you this in the ad account in a moment. You would essentially create duplications of the same exact ad set, launching it at the same exact budget, and in many ways sometimes you're not even changing any variable, sometimes you're straight up just duplicating the same thing, and Facebook based on the pacing algorithm will find the sweet spot. Now you will sometimes find stalling issues with this because obviously you're duplicating the same exact audience, so it may sometimes cause stalling issues where one of those ad sets is not feeding out properly, it's causing a little bit of stall. But if you duplicate four or five times, you'll find that a few of those ad sets will actually perform really, really well. Now the other thing that you normally do when you are using the horizontal method is you would test out a different variable. So that could include testing out different placements, it could include testing out different bid amounts, and it could include even different countries for example. And you can go so far as to test out all of those at once. So there's really no limit in how many of those you can duplicate. Now typically what we find for this is that an audience size of at least 200k and up is required for this to work properly. If your audience size is under 200k, normally you're going to start to run into a lot of those de-duping issues. Likewise, if you have another ad account that is also targeting the same exact people from the same Facebook page, the horizontal method can sometimes cause cannibalization of the campaigns where you're competing against each other. So just be aware of that.

## [06:02 - 09:06]

So let's go inside the ad account and I'll show you how the horizontal and the vertical method might work. Alright, so I'm inside of the ad account here and you can see here this is one of our rapid fire conversion objective campaigns. Now this one is really fresh. It's only spent about $128 and we've had it up for maybe four days. We've already got a row of 1.86. This is top of funnel so it's fantastic. And average cost per website purchase conversions at $30 at the ad set levels. It's coming in at $17 and then again at the ad level it's coming in at $5 for those purchases. So what we may want to do here is go fantastic. This is working really well. Let's go ahead and duplicate this and test, you know, the client may be saying, listen, we have the capability to scale, we've got the stock, we can handle it, let's scale this, we want more sales, right? So typically the standard way and the safe way to do that which is the vertical scaling method is to simply come in here and some of you may see an adjust budget button. However, they started to remove this and you basically scale by about 16 to 20% every two to three days. Now a good benchmark here as well if the vertical scaling method is going to work for you is looking at this graph if it does show up for you. So if it's a fairly flat line like this, which this one is, it means it's not a very scalable audience meaning the more money I spend, my CPA starts to go up. So that is something to be aware of is that the more I spend, the higher my CPA is likely to become. So for example here I'm spending $23 budget and Facebook is saying I should be getting 11 conversions but if I spent $45 I would only get 14 conversions. So that's not a very big increase on conversions for almost adding a significant amount to that small budget. So just be aware of that. What you want to see is a nice rising line which means you have a very scalable audience. So this is one of the ways to scale. You can also create an automation rule where Facebook will automatically go up and increase your budget as long as it's hitting certain metrics. Now if we're going to do the horizontal scaling method, we're going to basically duplicate the ad set and we can create four copies of this. And then what you would do is just go through each one individually and what you could do is decide to test on one of those variables. So a bit amount and if you're not sure what to do there just watch the manual bidding training. So for example here we could say we're going to test out a manual bid of $40 for example.

## [09:06 - 10:57]

We could go down here and edit what needs to be edited. On and on we would do this with each one of our ad sets. We could also do this with another variable such as placements. And you kind of want to move fast when you're running this kind of scaling strategy because obviously right away we have essentially quadrupled our daily ad spend. And so if you're not fast in killing the stuff that's not working you will obviously dramatically increase the client's CPA and they may not be very happy about that. So you do want to make sure that you are checking in within at least 12 hours of launching this kind of scaling strategy. And you should be able to see pretty soon through your CTR, your CPCs as well as your ultimate metrics like ad to carts and purchases if it's working out for you. So you would basically look at what am I getting now in terms of a cost per purchase through my existing ad set. And if any of these are going above that you would essentially kill it. You'd call it. You could test it out later on with another variable but you just want to let the other stuff try out. Another thing when you're using this method is that you may want to test out some different creative inside of those ad sets. It just seems to work really well. So roll out some more creative, maybe some of the stuff that you use during your rapid fire testing, pull it into this and see if any of those perform differently. Especially if you're just straight up duplicating without testing any other variables it's worth adding some different creative in there. And so this is the two scaling strategies that are most common with e-commerce.
